sources.title: State of Nevada Board of Wildlife Commissioners — Fishing Seasons CR 25-16 (Jan 1, 2026 – Dec 31, 2027)
sources.quotedText: Washoe Lake. Open year around. Daily: 10 game fish of which not more than 5 may be catfish. Possession: 20 game fish of which not more than 10 may be catfish. Any hour of the day or night.

sources.title: Washoe Lake Angler and Fish Survey Information — NDOW
sources.quotedText: Washoe Lake is a very shallow, productive water body located between Reno and Carson City. Washoe Lake covers an area of 5,800 acres when full and the big lake has a maximum depth of 12 feet. It is managed as a warm water fishery and contains White Bass, Sacramento Perch, Channel Catfish, Brown Bullhead, Carp, Wipers (White Bass x Striped Bass hybrid), and White Crappie. No restrictions on boats, however, at Washoe Lake State Park Boat Ramps: All boat harbors and other areas designated by buoys are zones in which a vessel must be operated at a speed that leaves a flat wake, but in no case may a vessel be operated at a speed in excess of 5 nautical miles per hour. Scripps Wildlife Management Area is closed to all trespass February 1 through June 30. Due to elevated methylmercury levels, the Nevada Department of Health and Human Services, Division of Public and Behavioral Health has issued a health advisory that recommends no consumption of white bass from Little and Big Washoe lakes. Season is open year around, any hour of the day or night. Limit is 10 game fish of which not more than 5 may be catfish. 750 Largemouth Bass were stocked in 2024 in Washoe and Little Washoe Lakes with an additional 200-700 more expected to be stocked in 2025.

sources.title: Washoe Lake — Nevada Department of Wildlife
sources.quotedText: Washoe Lake covers an area of 5,800 acres when full and the big lake has a maximum depth of 12 feet. It is managed as a warm water fishery and contains white bass, Sacramento perch, channel catfish, brown bullhead, carp, wipers (white bass x striped bass hybrid), and white crappie. A very shallow, productive water body located between Reno and Carson City. The area is comprised of Big Washoe Lake, Little Washoe Lake, and a large marsh (Scripps Wildlife Management Area) connecting the two.

sources.title: Apply & Buy — Nevada Fishing Licenses — NDOW
sources.quotedText: Anyone 12 years of age or older is required to have a fishing license in their possession while in the field.

regulations.humanSummary: Washoe Lake is open to fishing year-round.

sub_limits.note: Catfish (channel catfish and brown bullhead) count toward, and are capped within, the overall 10 daily / 20 possession game-fish limit — they are not an additional/separate limit.

parameters.counting_rule: Washoe Lake has a higher overall game-fish limit than the standard Washoe County Community Fishing Ponds: 10 game fish daily / 20 in possession, of which not more than 5 daily / 10 in possession may be catfish. No other species-specific sub-limit (e.g. for Sacramento perch, white bass, or largemouth bass) is stated in NDOW's current regulation table or its own angler guide for Washoe Lake.

regulations.humanSummary: Daily limit 10 game fish, of which not more than 5 may be catfish; possession limit 20 game fish, of which not more than 10 may be catfish, at Washoe Lake.
regulations.verbatimText: Daily: 10 game fish of which not more than 5 may be catfish. Possession: 20 game fish of which not more than 10 may be catfish.

parameters.note: Washoe Lake, unlike the Washoe County Community Fishing Ponds, is not restricted to park-open hours; NDOW's commission-regulation table and its own angler guide both state fishing is permitted any hour of the day or night.

regulations.humanSummary: Fishing at Washoe Lake is allowed any hour of the day or night.
regulations.verbatimText: Any hour of the day or night

parameters.note: Standard Nevada resident/nonresident fishing license required for anglers 12 and older; no water-specific permit exists for Washoe Lake.

regulations.humanSummary: A Nevada fishing license is required for anglers age 12 and older at Washoe Lake; no license is required under age 12.
regulations.verbatimText: Anyone 12 years of age or older is required to have a fishing license in their possession while in the field.

parameters.note: NDOW states there are no general boating restrictions on Washoe Lake, but within Washoe Lake State Park boat harbors and any other areas designated by buoys, vessels must be operated at a no-wake speed and in no case faster than 5 nautical miles per hour.

regulations.citation: Washoe Lake Angler and Fish Survey Information (NDOW)
regulations.humanSummary: No general boating restrictions apply on Washoe Lake, but a 5 mph no-wake speed limit applies within designated boat harbor/buoy zones at Washoe Lake State Park.
regulations.verbatimText: No restrictions on boats, however, at Washoe Lake State Park Boat Ramps: All boat harbors and other areas designated by buoys are zones in which a vessel must be operated at a speed that leaves a flat wake, but in no case may a vessel be operated at a speed in excess of 5 nautical miles per hour.

parameters.note: Partial-area closure with a seasonal window stated in the source text: the Scripps Wildlife Management Area — the large marsh connecting Big Washoe Lake and Little Washoe Lake — is closed to all trespass (including fishing access through it) from February 1 through June 30 each year. The main body of Washoe Lake is not closed by this rule. Stated in NDOW's Washoe Lake angler guide but not itself a line item in the NBWC CR 25-16 commission-regulation table, so treated as medium confidence.

regulations.citation: Washoe Lake Angler and Fish Survey Information (NDOW)
regulations.humanSummary: The Scripps Wildlife Management Area marsh connecting Big and Little Washoe Lake is closed to all trespass (including fishing) from February 1 through June 30.
regulations.verbatimText: Scripps Wildlife Management Area is closed to all trespass February 1 through June 30th. Please keep from fishing in this area during this time.

parameters.description: Nevada Division of Public and Behavioral Health consumption advisory: due to elevated methylmercury levels, no consumption of white bass caught from Little Washoe Lake or Big (Washoe) Lake is recommended. This is a public-health advisory relayed via NDOW's angler guide, not a take/possession regulation issued by NDOW itself, and NDOW directs anglers to its website for current advisory status.

raw.issuing_body: Nevada Department of Health and Human Services, Division of Public and Behavioral Health

regulations.citation: Washoe Lake Angler and Fish Survey Information (NDOW), citing NV DHHS advisory
regulations.humanSummary: State health officials advise against eating white bass caught from Washoe Lake or Little Washoe Lake due to elevated methylmercury levels; this is a consumption advisory, not a catch/possession limit.
regulations.verbatimText: Due to elevated methylmercury levels, the Nevada Department of Health and Human Services, Division of Public and Behavioral Health has issued a health advisory that recommends no consumption of white bass from Little and Big Washoe lakes. Please visit NDOW's web site (www.ndow.org) for more information.
